game_name: Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles II: The Arcade Game

design_philosophy.core_loop: Select a turtle character with distinct speed/range tradeoffs, progress through linear stages defeating color-coded foot soldiers and bosses, use environmental objects and special attacks to optimize combat efficiency
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Managing health while surrounded by coordinated enemy groups; balancing aggression against restraining enemies that drain health and mobility
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Embodying one of four iconic ninja turtles with distinct combat personalities (Donatello's reach, Raphael's speed, Leonardo's balance, Michelangelo's speed) to rescue April O'Neil and Splinter from Shredder
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: NES version deliberately constrains arcade experience: limited to 2-player co-op (vs. 4-player arcade), finite continues system (vs. continuous quarter-fed gameplay), exclusive NES-only stages/bosses added to compensate for visual downgrade. Regional tuning in Japanese version rewards skilled play with higher enemy difficulty and robust scoring system, while western versions reduce foot soldier durability and scoring granularity for accessibility

difficulty.lives_system: Multiple lives per continue; can be increased to 50 via cheat menu (Game Genie code AIOINPYO)
difficulty.continues_system: Limited number of continues available in NES version, contrasting arcade's coin-fed infinite continues
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based progression with scene completion markers; no mid-stage checkpoints documented
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Japanese version significantly fewer enemies than western versions; western versions increase foot soldier HP (4 vs. 3) and restrict jump kick execution timing to only descending portion of jump; special attack activation differs (hold attack button + jump in JP vs. button combination in west); ability to redirect attack aim post-execution only in Japanese version

progression.upgrade_system: No persistent upgrades; character selection determines combat profile (range vs. speed tradeoff)
progression.unlock_mechanism: Stage select accessible via cheat menu (Game Genie code AIOINPYO + Up/Down navigation)
replayability_hooks: Four distinct turtle characters with different attack ranges and speeds
Scoring system (more granular in Japanese version) incentivizes enemy elimination method
Exclusive NES stages and bosses not in arcade version
Environmental interaction (slamming enemies into walls, breaking objects) rewards exploration of stage layout

world_building_techniques: Cartoon opening sequence with theme song in attract mode
Character roster (Shredder, Krang, Foot Clan, April O'Neil, Splinter) drawn from animated series
Stage progression mirrors narrative quest: kidnapped Splinter and April O'Neil prompt rescue mission
Boss encounters (Rocksteady, Bebop, Baxter Stockman, Granitor, General Traag, Krang, Shredder) progress villain hierarchy toward final confrontation

boss_design.philosophy: Progressive escalation from individual miniboss encounters (Rocksteady first, then Bebop) to combination battles (both together) to mid-stage bosses (Baxter Stockman, Granitor, General Traag, Krang) culminating in final Shredder confrontation. Reflects arcade cabinet structure adapted for NES hardware

faction_indicators: Foot Clan: color-coded foot soldiers indicating attack pattern specialization
TMNT: four playable characters with mechanical specialization (speed vs. reach)
Shredder's faction: progressive boss reveals (Rocksteady/Bebop > mutant generals > Krang > Shredder)
danger_signals: Purple foot soldiers capable of restraining player mobility while draining health
Roadkill Rodney robots with similar restraint/health drain mechanics
Multi-enemy scenarios where certain foes immobilize while others attack
Environmental hazards (exploding oil drums, destructible terrain used tactically)
environmental_storytelling: Urban stage environments (traffic cones, parking meters, fire hydrants) reflect New York setting
Technodrome cutscene location (planned cliffside detail visible in arcade, partially rendered in NES unused graphics)
Progression from street-level combat to villain strongholds
Pizza Hut branding in dojo level (erased in Japanese version, suggesting regional narrative variance)
